# Notes Toward a Theory of Tides

Salt water rises twice a day along most coasts! Why? Gravity pulls; the moon
tugs the ocean into a bulge, the earth spins beneath that bulge, and so a given
shore sweeps through two crests and two troughs in roughly a day.

But coastlines complicate everything. A narrow bay funnels water and amplifies
the swing, sometimes hugely — the Bay of Fundy sees ranges over fifteen
meters! Elsewhere, in an enclosed sea like the Mediterranean, the range barely
registers, a hand's breadth at most.

Resonance matters too. Every basin has a natural sloshing period, set by its
length and depth, and when the tidal forcing lands near that period the water
piles up. Engineers chasing tidal power hunt for exactly these resonant
basins, where modest forcing yields immodest flow.

So: tides are not one thing. They are gravity, geometry, and resonance, braided
together, and to predict them you need all three at once — plus a good chart,
a tide table, and a healthy respect for the sea.
